0: <title>Modern analysis of an ancient integrated farming arrangement: life cycle assessment of a mulberry dyke and pond system </title>
Miguel F. Astudillo 1 . Gunnar Thalwitz Fritz Vollrath 1
Received: 30 January 2015 / Accepted: 7 August 2015/Published online: 18 August 2015 Springer-Verlag Berlin Heidelberg 2015

Abstract Purpose Integrated agriculture and aquaculture (IAA), as typ- ified by the mulberry dike-pond system (DPS) of the Pearl River delta of southern China, is often cited as an example of sustainable intensified production due to its characteristic closed loop recycling of nutrients. In this study, we tackle two issues that have been hardly addressed in previous analyses of aquaculture production, greenhouse gas emissions (GHGe) from the pond and the role of labour. Methods Previous assessments led us to revisit the sustain- ability of the DPS system as a model for a well-studied IAA system using a life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology. Our study quantifies on-farm CH4 and N2O emissions and indirect emissions embedded in inputs, using the global warming po- tential (GWP) metric. To model the indirect impact of the high labour requirements of the system, a simple methodology based on metabolizable energy requirements is proposed. Results and discussion Our GHGe assessment suggests that using fish ponds to treat organic waste results in higher net emissions than alternative waste processing options (e.g. composting), even when the co-production of fish is accounted for. The majority of total system GWP 100 (97 %) can be attributed to methane from the fertilised ponds. South China. Although the mulberry dike and pond system (DPS) as described by Ruddle and Zhong (1988) among others is no longer practised, it supported very high population densities over centuries. IAA systems are still widely used in the culture of cyprinids, which account for almost 40 % of total aquaculture output (Bostock et al. 2010), and have been suggested as a model for sustainable production (Naylor et al. 2000; Godfray et al. 2010). Here, we provide an overview of the Zhujiang delta DPS together with a review of existing sustainability assessments. Next, GHGe of the system are ex- amined using the life cycle assessment (LCA) framework, separating on-farm emissions and indirect emissions related to the energy demand for labour. Finally, the results are com- pared with current literature on IAA and intensive systems of silk and fish production.

South China. Although the mulberry dike and pond system (DPS) as described by Ruddle and Zhong (1988) among others is no longer practised, it supported very high population densities over centuries. IAA systems are still widely used in the culture of cyprinids, which account for almost 40 % of total aquaculture output (Bostock et al. 2010), and have been suggested as a model for sustainable production (Naylor et al. 2000; Godfray et al. 2010). Here, we provide an overview of the Zhujiang delta DPS together with a review of existing sustainability assessments. Next, GHGe of the system are ex- amined using the life cycle assessment (LCA) framework, separating on-farm emissions and indirect emissions related to the energy demand for labour. Finally, the results are com- pared with current literature on IAA and intensive systems of silk and fish production.
4: <header>1.1 Origins and evolution of the mulberry dike and pond system </header>

Silk production (sericulture) has its origins in the domestica- tion of the mulberry silkworm Bombyx mori in China around 5000 years ago. To produce silk, the monophagous silkworms are fed mulberry leaves for approximately 4 weeks until they are ready to spin a protective cocoon in which they undergo metamorphosis to the moth stage. Before the emergence of the silkmoth, the cocoons are unravelled (reeled) using hot water.

The mulberry DPS emerged when dike-pond systems used to control flooding and expand rice cultivation were combined with silkworm production. The development of these land reclamation techniques in the Zhujiang delta probably was the consequence of agricultural expansion upstream causing increased sediment loading (Lo 1996) and formation of new land in the delta.

Integration of aquaculture and agriculture emerged during the Tang dynasty, when fruit was grown on the dikes. Incorporation of mulberry trees spread during the 17th centu- ry, as the area's suitability for mulberry, allowed eight annual crop cycles (Lo 1996). Residues from cocoon production, such as uneaten leaves and pupae, were added to the pond along with animal manure and human excrement, sustaining phytoplankton and herbivorous fish. A polyculture of carps with different feeding habits made efficient use of nutrients at multiple trophic levels (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Organic residues accumulating at the bottom of the pond were re- moved to fertilise dike crops, recirculating nutrients.

With the opening of industrial silk processing facilities in the 19th century, rice was progressively substituted by mul- berry (Zhong 1982). Increasing access to inputs in the later 20th century evolved the system further, to incorporate exter- nal inputs such as concentrated feeds and synthetic fertilisers. Industrialisation of the Zhujiang delta led to the effective dis- appearance of this traditional system by the late 1990s. While the area devoted to aquaculture increased, the focus switched

to production of high-value species such as eel and terrapin, and today, inputs are largely sourced off-farm (Wong Chor Yee 1999). In general, the industrialisation of the delta has led to large increases in income but also increased pollution and environmental degradation (Lin 1997).

7: <header>1.2 Assessing sustainability in the DPS system </header>

The oil crisis of the 1970s motivated a series of studies of energy use in food production, pioneered by David Pimentel and colleagues (e.g. (Pimentel et al. 1973)). Two different approaches were used to aggregate the different forms of en- ergy input required to produce the final product. One was based on thermodynamics that introduced concepts such as emergy (Odum 1988), the other built on heuristic approaches based on "quality indices" using technology-dependent con- version efficiencies to reduce different forms of energy to a single metric, such as tons of oil equivalent (Giampietro 2006).

Both thermodynamic and heuristic-based approaches have been used to analyse silkworm and pond-based aquaculture. Using input-output energy balances similar to the ones cur- rently employed in LCA, Dazhong and Pimentel (1986) con- ducted a study of 17th century cocoon production in Jiaxing China and conclude that sericulture-based IAA was generally sustainable and particularly effective in recycling organic waste. To account for energy from human labour, Dhazong and Pimentel used the energy content of food. This energy requirement is small compared to that obtained from photo- synthesis; however, embodied energy from production of foodstuffs was not taken into account (Giampietro and Pimentel 1990). To give an example, the farm-gate cumulative energy demand (CED) of tilapia is approximately 16 times its metabolizable energy content (Pelletier and Tyedmers 2010). Emergy analyses suggest that sericulture's emergy sustainabil- ity index is superior to the average of Chinese agriculture (Chen et al. 2006b).

The most complete characterisation of DPS comes from extensive field work done in the 1980s summarised in Ruddle and Zhong (1988). Apparently, as a consequence of rural reforms, the use of external inputs was increasing and households relied on pig manure and concentrated feeds in- stead of silkworm litter to fertilise the ponds (Ruddle 1985). Nutrient balances suggest that at that point, the system could not be sustained without external inputs, which most likely entered the system as human food intake and feed for pigs (Edwards 1993). Analysis of the trophic dynamics in the pond revealed that high fish yields were driven by 'imports' of manure and concentrated feeds (Ruddle and Christensen 1993). These changes reflect the broader trend in Chinese agriculture towards specialisation, increased use of external inputs (Chen et al. 2006a) and higher economic rates of return.

LCA standards provide a consistent accounting framework for sustainability assessment, and LCA is now broadly used to analyse supply chains (Pelletier et al. 2011). To avoid the shortcomings of a "silver bullet" single indicator, several in- dices such as global warming potential (GWP), eutrophication potential or CED are usually characterised. This study consti- tutes the first LCA of the mulberry DPS system, aimed to clarify the potential environmental burden of these traditional IAA systems.

11: This study models the traditional DPS system of the Zhujiang delta with minimal external inputs. The objective is to shed light on the possible environmental burdens arising from this type of traditional IAA system, which is distinguished by a high rate of nutrient cycling. The analysis focuses on on-farm GHGe which can be expected to be the main source of GWP in systems with minimal inputs such as the DPS. Impact categories such as fossil fuel depletion, CED and eu- trophication are discussed qualitatively in section 4.5.

In LCA, the functional unit (FU) should represent the func- tions of the system studied. The mulberry DPS had several outputs yielding both edible and non-edible products, which complicates the selection of a single FU. For completeness, the FU employed here refers to all individual products provid- ed by 1 ha of DPS. Aggregated (i.e. non-allocated) GWP 100 is also provided. DPS outputs are silkworm cocoons, fish and some vegetables. The allocation of GWP between cocoons, fish and vegetables is detailed in section 2.2. The system boundary includes the area under DPS and upstream processes associated with the production of inputs. In the DPS, the dike and the pond occupied equal areas; thus, each hectare of DPS is composed of half a hectare of pond and half a hectare of dike (Ruddle et al. 1986). External inputs were restricted to the rice needed to meet energy demands for manual labour and minimal quantities of quicklime. In order to compare the use of sericultural waste as pond fertiliser with alternatives such as composting, a comparative assessment of GWP 100 for treat- ment of 1 t of sericultural waste was conducted. The details of the comparison are provided in section 2.6. The system is assumed to be in steady state; therefore, changes in C in pond sediments and soil were not considered.

The mulberry DPS has in effect disappeared but fortunately was relatively well documented. Most of the data on the sys- tem comes from studies by Ruddle and colleagues from 1982 to 1993, complemented by field experiments (Chang and Ouyang 1988) in similar systems. GWP100 of external inputs (Chinese rice and quicklime) were taken from the Ecoinvent v3.1 database. Emissions were inventoried on Simapro v.8,

using the IPCC 2013 GWP 100-year characterisation factors for GHG. Estimates for CH4 and N2O emissions are from literature values as detailed in section 2.5. Yields were adjust- ed for lower inputs using a simplified model of the DPS sub- system; the model is detailed in section 2.3. All parameters are available in the Electronic Supplementary Material. An out- line of mass flows is presented in Fig. 1.

14: For systems with multiple outputs such as the DPS, different allocation methods are available. To increase robustness, this study employed several allocation approaches with preference for "avoided burden" as outlined by the European Commission (2010). In the avoided burden approach, the en- vironmental impact of co-products is approximated by equiv- alent processes and subtracted from the total burden of the system. Accordingly, the GWP 100 of cocoons was calculated by subtracting the burden of vegetables and fish from total system GWP 100, using estimates from comparable systems. GWP1 of vegetables was approximated using the module for celery in Ecoinvent 3.1, and of fish production using pub- lished values from other IAA systems (Phong et al. 2011). Burdens were also allocated on the basis of metabolizable energy, nitrogen content and monetary value of products exported from the system. Rural China depended largely on locally available biofuels such as firewood and straw, often in short supply. However, rural household use of coal rapidly expanded after the 1970 (Xiaohua and Zhenmin 2001). We assumed that non-leaf mulberry biomass replaced coal at household level and is treated as avoided burden under all allocation approaches.
15: Fish yields in our study were based on literature values for conversion of input of excreta and silk rearing waste to bio- mass. Ratios of manure application to fish yield have been shown to be linear over a range of amounts applied (Schroeder 1978; Zhu et al. 1990). We assumed a ratio of 8.3 kg manure (dry basis) per kg of fish live weight as esti- mated for Chinese polyculture systems (Zhu et al. 1990). These values are broadly comparable to those estimated for IAA systems in the Mekong Delta (Nhan et al. 2008a). Major inputs to the pond are waste products of sericulture in the form of rearing waste and pupae and human excreta. The amount of excreta applied to the pond was estimated from labour require- ments per hectare. Ten percent of the dike area was dedicated to elephant grass (Ruddle et al. 1986). Based on a trophic model of DPS (Ruddle and Christensen 1993), 25 % of ele- phant grass and 80 % of vegetable biomass were modelled as direct inputs to grass carp, with a feed conversion ratio of fresh inputs to live weight of 50:1. Excreta from grass carp was
Fig. 1 Mass and energy flows in the mulberry DPS system (dry basis)
treated as an input of manure to the system. The feed conver- sion ratio of silkworm pupae to fish (fresh basis) was 5:1, estimate of Coche et al. (1996).

Mulberry yields were estimated using a quadratic fertiliser response function based on long-term fertilisation experi- ments (CSRTI 2013). Pond sediments were assumed to accu- mulate 65 of the nitrogen applied to the pond (Gross et al. 2000), which is again comparable to results from IAA systems in the Mekong delta (Nhan et al. 2008b). Silkworm yields were based on observations from the Zhujiang delta (Ruddle and Zhong 1988).

In our study, woody biomass production from mulberry trees was estimated using the model detailed in previous work (Astudillo et al. 2014) adjusted for the differing humidity val- ue reported for Chinese mulberry (Lu al. 2009). On the basis of calorific value, woody biomass offsets the consumption of 2.3 t of hard coal per ha of DPS.
16: Fish yields in our study were based on literature values for conversion of input of excreta and silk rearing waste to bio- mass. Ratios of manure application to fish yield have been shown to be linear over a range of amounts applied (Schroeder 1978; Zhu et al. 1990). We assumed a ratio of 8.3 kg manure (dry basis) per kg of fish live weight as esti- mated for Chinese polyculture systems (Zhu et al. 1990). These values are broadly comparable to those estimated for IAA systems in the Mekong Delta (Nhan et al. 2008a). Excreta from grass carp was
Fig. 1 Mass and energy flows in the mulberry DPS system (dry basis)
treated as an input of manure to the system. The feed conver- sion ratio of silkworm pupae to fish (fresh basis) was 5:1, estimate of Coche et al. (1996).

Mulberry yields were estimated using a quadratic fertiliser response function based on long-term fertilisation experi- ments (CSRTI 2013). Pond sediments were assumed to accu- mulate 65 of the nitrogen applied to the pond (Gross et al. 2000), which is again comparable to results from IAA systems in the Mekong delta (Nhan et al. 2008b). Silkworm yields were based on observations from the Zhujiang delta (Ruddle and Zhong 1988).

In our study, woody biomass production from mulberry trees was estimated using the model detailed in previous work (Astudillo et al. 2014) adjusted for the differing humidity val- ue reported for Chinese mulberry (Lu al. 2009). On the basis of calorific value, woody biomass offsets the consumption of 2.3 t of hard coal per ha of DPS.
17: Very high labour intensity was a hallmark of the DPS, and import of food into the system has been suggested as a major inflow of nutrients (Edwards 1993). Thus, off-farm GHGe related to food imported to sustain the physically demanding work are a potentially significant source of indirect GHGe. An outline framework for estimating these emissions is presented below.

With some exceptions, the impact of human labour is gen- erally not considered in LCA studies (Rugani et al. 2012). When included, it is typically aggregated with other forms of energy implicitly equiparating them (Ndong et al. 2009; Held et al. 2013). Alternatively, Rugani et al. (2012) proposed a framework to evaluate the impact of labour using input- output tables, taking into account processes and services as- sociated with labour input (e.g. food, clothes, housing). This is related to the proposition of Giampietro and Pimentel (1990),

who suggested expanding system boundaries to account for inputs necessary to support human labour. We propose here a simplified method that distinguishes between energy (i) directly required for specific tasks and (ii) required to meet base metabolic needs. Like Held et al. (2013), we, too, limited impacts from labour to the food re- quired to meet energy demands above the basal metabolic rate. Hourly energy expenditure on each task was based on the "physical activity ratio" of similar activities, specified by gender (FAO 2004).

We then quantified the amount of food needed to obtain the required energy, based on a simplified representative diet and the metabolic energy of its components (FAO 1972). According to household surveys (Du et al. 2014), animal sourced energy intake in China in 1992 was under 8 %, while grains accounted for 81 %. For the sake of simplicity, we assumed that 92 % of the metabolizable energy was obtained from rice, the staple food of southern China, and 8 % in form of fish produced on farm. GHGe from Chinese rice are from the Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
18: Very high labour intensity was a hallmark of the DPS, and import of food into the system has been suggested as a major inflow of nutrients (Edwards 1993). Thus, off-farm GHGe related to food imported to sustain the physically demanding work are a potentially significant source of indirect GHGe. An outline framework for estimating these emissions is presented below.

For the sake of simplicity, we assumed that 92 % of the metabolizable energy was obtained from rice, the staple food of southern China, and 8 % in form of fish produced on farm. GHGe from Chinese rice are from the Ecoinvent v3.1 database.
19: The major sources of direct GHGe in agriculture are dinitrogen monoxide (N20), and methane (CH4), the latter associated primarily with livestock husbandry. In this case, mulberry was only fertilised with extracted pond sediments. We estimated N2O emissions from the mulberry grown on dikes using the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's (IPCC) Tier 1 approach (De Klein et al. 2006), ac- counting for direct pathways (volatilisation) and indirect path- ways (leaching and atmospheric deposition). The large major- ity of GWP100 in cocoon production is attributable to mulber- ry cultivation and sericulture waste management (Astudillo et al. 2014). We thus assigned no direct GHGe to silkworm rearing.

Direct CH4 and N2O emissions are rarely accounted for in LCAs of aquaculture due to a lack of available data (Bosma et al. 2011). However, they may contribute significantly to global warming (Williams and Crutzen 2010; Henriksson et al. 2014), in particular for unaerated fertilised ponds in semi-intensive aquaculture such as described here.
20: Only a relatively small proportion of N in diets is assimilated into fish biomass. Unassimilated N is excreted largely as am- monia and subsequently oxidised in nitrification and denitri- fication processes. Incomplete nitrification and denitrification result in N2O emissions through a number of possible path- ways, as detailed in Hu et al. (2013). These emissions are a function of applied N, mediated by locally specific factors varying over time, such as pH, water temperature and

dissolved oxygen (DO) concentrations. Unfortunately, pub- lished measurements of N2O emissions from aquaculture sys- tems are sparse. Evidence from eutrophic rivers suggests that emissions are strongly and positively correlated with temper- ature and nitrate concentration (Hinshaw and Dahlgren 2013) and may have been severely underestimated (Turner et al. 2015). Aubin et al. (2006) provide first estimates of N2O emissions in intensive turbot production and emphasise the need for quantification. Importantly, their study pointed to aquaculture organic residues (sludge) as a potential source of N2O. In experiments on catfish supplied with commercial feeds, 1.3 % of applied N was emitted as N2O (Hu et al. 2013), with emissions increasing at lower DO concentrations and higher feeding rates. Comparable results come from rice- fish systems in Cuttack, India, where 1.2% of N applied in the form of urea was lost as N2O (Datta et al. 2009). In our study, we employed an emission factor (EF) of 1.3 % of N2O-N as the experiments conducted by Hu et al. (2013) were under similar pH and temperature conditions (Chang and Ouyang 1988). The indirect pathway of deposition of volatilised am- monia was based on estimates of pond ammonia emissions (Gross et al. 2000).

22: CH4 emissions occur during anaerobic mineralisation of or- ganic carbon (OC). In the DPS, OC enters the pond through inputs of elephant grass and waste, and through the photosyn- thetic activity of phytoplankton. Removal pathways for OC are burial in sediments, respiration, fixation in fish biomass and mineralisation through aerobic and anaerobic processes.

OC mineralisation begins in the aerobic zone, where poly- mers are hydrolysed and a proportion is oxidised into CO2 (Blackburn 1987). Aerobic oxidation of C typically accounts for 50° of total carbon mineralisation in fertilised fish ponds (Riise and Roos 1997). Once oxygen is depleted, nitrate is used as an electron acceptor in the nitrification- denitrification process, resulting in CO2, N20 and N2. Further, carbon mineralisation is dominated by methanogenesis and sulphate reduction (Blackburn 1987; Riise and Roos 1997). While sulphate reduction is important in saline environments, it is a minor pathway in freshwater fish ponds (Schroeder 1987a; Riise and Roos 1997). Following fermentation processes, acetoclastic and hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis leads to increased levels of CO2, H2O and CH4. The methane can be oxidised by methanotrophic bacteria in the water column and sediment interfaces (Bastviken et al. 2008). Total CH4 emissions are thus substantially determined by total input of OC less seques- tration in sediments, % of OC assimilated by fish, % of OC aerobically mineralised, % of OC mineralised via methanogenesis and % of CH4 oxidised in the water column and sediments prior to emission.

The dominance of certain pathways in carbon cycling de- pends on factors such as farm practices, local climate and biochemical characteristics of soil and water. Low tempera- tures are known to inhibit methanogenesis, but since pond water temperatures tend to be above 18 °C for most of the year (Ruddle and Zhong 1988), we assumed that temperature did not limit methanogenic activity. Higher organic loads pro- vide more substrate for methanogenesis and reduce DO levels and have been reported as accounting for higher CH4 emis- sions in rice paddies (Cai et al. 2007). Here, we assumed CH4 emissions to be a linear function of organic load. Bioturbation by benthic fish in rice paddies has been found to increase CH4 emissions, possibly due to increased ebullition and decreased DO levels, which favour methanogenesis (Frei and Becker 2005). Increasing stocking density of fish is thus likely to increase methane emission rates due to lower DO levels and benthic activity. For this study, the fraction of carbon mineralised via methanogenesis and the fraction of non- mineralised carbon was based on experimental results from fertilised polycultured ponds in Thailand (Riise and Roos 1997) operated under conditions similar to the system studied. Methane oxidation is a complex process that can be influ- enced by several factors, notably the presence of reducers and the transport mechanism. Methane can be oxidised anaerobi- cally either through sulphate reduction or mediated by denitrifying bacteria (Ettwig et al. 2010). It can be also oxidised aerobically, a pathway that has been shown to be dominant-by more than an order of magnitude-in oligotro- phic lakes (Deutzmann and Schink 2011). The presence of sulphates is expected to be low in this environment, and re- ported pH levels are fairly constant (Chang and Ouyang 1988); thus, oxygen availability is likely to be key in CH4 oxidation. Oxygen dynamics in ponds differ substantially from those in many other freshwater environments, being shallower and smaller in area, with limited water circulation (Chang and Ouyang 1988) and a higher respirating biomass. DO levels vary between day and night due to photosynthetic activity of phytoplankton (Chang and Ouyang 1988) and a lack of supplementary aeration. Therefore, photosynthetic ac- tivity is likely to be a key factor in oxidation rates. For this study, we assumed a methane oxidation rate of 0%, observed in wastewater unaerated oxidation ponds of similar depth (Detweiler et al. 2014). In both, retention and fish pond meth- ane are mainly emitted via ebullition (Xiao et al. 2014), hence with similar retention times. Photosynthetic activity increases diurnal DO levels both in the fish and oxidation ponds. However, the absence of fish in oxidation ponds results in comparatively higher DO levels, possibly leading to an over- estimation of CH4 oxidation rates, which are much higher than those found in shallow areas of oligotrophic lakes (Bastviken et al. 2008; Bussmann et al. 2013).
Primary production (PP) in fishponds is regulated by a large number of factors (Tucker et al. 2008). Ruddle and

Zhong (1988) report net PP of 22.4 t dm phytoplankton/ha/ year in DPS ponds, equivalent to 3 g day. These esti- mates are within the normal range for intensively fertilised ponds (Schroeder 1987b) and were used in our model. Percentages of PP contributing directly to detritus are from Ruddle and Christensen (1993) and are similar to values pro- posed for manured ponds in Israel (Schroeder 1987a). OC from inputs was estimated from mass balances and elemental composition of constituents. The parameters above are all subject to significant uncer- tainty, and variability determined by location- and management-specific factors. They represent at this point a 'best guess'. To analyse the robustness of the result, an uncer- tainty analysis was performed; details are provided in section 2.5.3.

26: CH4 emissions are a function of available organic carbon and benthic dynamics, and reported emissions vary widely. Primary data for CH4 emissions from DPS are not available, and the estimates below are based on data from measurements in other tropical freshwater ponds. Following Weidema et al. (2013), the inherent or "basic" uncertainty was estimated as the variance of sampled data. In the absence of sufficient data to fit a distribution, it was assumed that emissions are log- normally distributed, which is a common assumption in LCA (Henriksson et al. 2013). The data used as proxy were from measurements on 52 ponds in tropical India (Selvam et al. 2014), which we expected to capture the spatial and temporal variability of CH4 emissions. The coefficient of variation reported by Selvam et al. (2014) is slightly higher than the one found in a tropical shallow fishpond in China by Xiao et al. (2014), which was a study of one pond without assessing inter-pond variability.

In our study, the uncertainty in methane emissions related to "unrepresentativeness" was estimated using a pedigree ma- trix. The unrepresentativeness was quantified following Weidema et al. (2013) and a 90 confidence interval estimat- ed using NumPy (van der Walt et al. 2011). The quality indi- ces are appended in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

28: <header>2.6 GHGe of alternative uses of organic waste </header>

One of the most common treatments of organic waste from sericulture is composting (Astudillo et al. 2014). In order to examine whether the use of sericultural waste as pond fertiliser is a superior waste treatment process from a climate change mitigation perspective, a comparative assessment was conducted. The net emissions of treating 1 t of organic waste were calculated using the avoided burden approach. Gross emissions from the pond were based on EF from this study, and emissions from composting came from Amlinger et al.

(2008). To facilitate comparability, emissions associated to labour were not considered. Compost and pond sediments were modelled as substitutes for synthetic fertilisers, and avoided burdens of fish production were credited based on comparable production systems (Phong et al. 2011). For this comparison, only the avoided N was considered, as the GWP of N fertilisers is significantly higher than for P and K fertilisers.
29: Total labour requirements as reported by Ruddle and Zhong (1988) were higher than previous estimates (Dazhong and Pimentel 1986), at approximately 11,885 h/ha/year. As Fig. 2 shows, the majority of labour demand originated from the dike sub-system. Women contributed more time but slight- ly less energy to the system as the majority of physically demanding tasks were done by men. At 9 h of labour per person-day, average additional food intake required is 1651 kcal/day for men and 1296 kcal/day for women.
30: Annual missions from 1 ha of DPS were calculated as 15 t CO2eq with 89 % originating on-farm. The remainder was a function of imported inputs, with rice and quicklime respon- sible for 8 and 3 % of total GWP10 respectively. Emissions related to labour (rice plus fish consumption) ranged from 22 % under energy allocation to 11 % (avoided burden) (Fig. 3). Woody biomass output substituting fossil fuels re- duced total GWP100 by between 16% (economic allocation) and 22 % (avoided burden). By far, the largest source of GWP 100 was methane from the pond subsystem, responsible for 97 % of total gross GWP100, followed by N20 emissions from the pond and dike. Of those, 82 % were emissions from the pond, and 18 % could be attributed to the dike. Indirect N2O emissions played a small role, contributing 13 to total N2O emissions. Under all allocation approaches, the majority of impact was attributed to silkworm cocoons; impact was similar across economic, energy and nitrogen allocation (Fig. 4).

Compared to alternative sericulture waste management systems such as composting, net emissions per unit mass of organic waste would be higher when processed in semi- intensive aquaculture systems as described here (Tables 1 and 2). Pond GHGe, in particular of CH4, outweigh the avoided burden of fish produced, even in aquaculture systems with high environmental impact.
The uncertainty analysis yielded 5th and 95th percentiles at 187 and 1102 kg CH4/ha DPS/year, respectively. Average
Fig. 2 Labour energy expenditure by subsystem and gender

methane emissions were 533 kg CH4/ha DPS/year. The im- pact of uncertainty over the GWP100 of the DPS system is presented in Table 3. For comparative purposes, results omit- ting emissions associated to labour are also presented.

1: The DPS system modelled here was highly labour intensive, and, due to multifunctionality and small size of holdings, it would be resistant to mechanisation. Indirect impacts of la- bour accounted for up to 22 % of total GWP 100 (Fig. 3), ap- proximately 3.3 t CO2eq/ha/year of the system. The majority of this would be due to the large amount of labour in dike management, which may partially explain why the system evolved towards intensified aquaculture rather than agricul- ture. The relative impact is higher than the burden from labour in the agri-food sector in a European context (Rugani et al. 2012), even where these effects were defined more tightly here than in Rugani et al. (2012).

In agricultural systems, where cost structures are such that substitutability between mechanisation and increased labour input is feasible, accounting for the potentially significant en- ergy costs of manual fieldwork allows a more accurate com- parison across production options. The DPS example illus- trates how mechanisation can potentially reduce
Fig. 3 Share of GWP 100 attributable to field emissions, labour and other inputs under different allocation approaches

environmental burdens of agriculture through increased pro- ductivity. Increased mechanisation could nonetheless indirect- ly foster CO2 emissions from, e.g., land conversion due to rebound effects (Lambin and Meyfroidt 2011). However, these potential impacts were beyond the scope of this study, and a consequential LCA approach would be better suited to investigate them. It should also be noted that, with human diet differing over time and location, the impact of labour estimat- ed here should be treated as a 'snapshot' of the DPS at a specific moment and place. Generally, metabolic energy re- quirements can be used to estimate food intake from physical activity data. However, it is not an ideal sole indicator, due to the significant potential trade-offs between healthy and "ener- gy-efficient" diets. For instance, diets rich in sugars are energy-dense and have comparatively low GHGe per caloric input but would not be advisable health-wise.

3: <header>4.2 Modelling direct GHG emissions </header>

Few aquaculture studies have characterised on-farm N2O emissions (Bosma et al. 2011; Hu et al. 2013), and results presented here should be interpreted as a first approximation. N2O production is dependent on location and system-specific factors (Kampschreur et al. 2009; Hinshaw and Dahlgren 2013). The tentative use of an N2O emission factor of 1.3% results in emissions around 300 kg CO2eq per ton of fish. This EF is based on intensive production with pelleted feed, where nitrogen conversion efficiency is generally higher (Edwards 1993). N20 emissions are thus likely to be an underestimate, as Turner et al (2015) suggest may be the case for streams in areas of intense agricultural production as well.

As for N20, data on methane emissions are also lacking for aquaculture (Bosma et al. 2011; Henriksson et al. 2014). Measurements from integrated rice-fish systems were used to analyse IAA in the Mekong Delta and dominated the GWP100 of the fish subsystem (Phong et al. 2011). In our study, methane emissions from ponds played a larger role and dominated farm GWP100- Even using lower estimates (Table 3), GWP10 of methane emissions were an order of magnitude higher than that of GWP100 of N2O emissions, suggesting CH4 from fertilised ponds to be the determining factor in total GWP10

To the best of our knowledge, surveys on average CH4 emissions from ponds in subtropical China have not been conducted. Instead, we compared the average emissions with results from an extensive survey of inland waters in tropical India (Panneer Selvam et al. 2014). The average emissions per area of pond according to our estimates (18 mmol/m2/day) would be comparable to the average emissions from freshwater ponds in tropical India (17.9 mmol/m2/day). However, the survey observed a large spread over CH4 emissions in ponds, which formed the basis of our uncertainty analysis. Despite the
Fig. 4 GWP 100 of DPS products under different allocation approaches
economic
energy
nitrogen
avoided burden
fish
vegetables
cocoons
uncertainty, CH4 concentrations from ponds were signifi- cantly higher than in lakes, reservoirs and rivers, which according to Panner Selvam et al. (2014) might be caused by higher organic loads.

There are considerable uncertainties in our own esti- mates as the result of combining and integrating a wide range literature sources. We attempted to model uncer- tainty, but more work is needed especially regarding in- herent uncertainty and the effects of changes in opera- tional parameters. Our difficulties were two-fold: first, attempts to establish deterministic models relating key variables such as PP and fertiliser application are gener- ally unsuccessful (Oláh et al. 1986; Diana et al. 1991), and second, IAA systems are typically small scale and function under a large range of operational parameters, which are difficult to predict yet increase temporal and spatial variability.

While the specific DPS system analysed here is appar- ently no longer an economically viable activity in the Zhujiang delta, the potential contribution to global warming from similar production systems may be signif- icant and worth greater attention. Nearly 6.6 MMT of bighead and silver carps are produced annually in China, fed on leftovers of polyculture systems or using pond fertilisation (Cao et al. 2015). In this production, organic
fertilisation is common (Yang 2005a, b) and could be responsible for significant CH4 emissions.

7: According to our study, the average GWP 100 of the products from the DPS system appeared to be higher compared with similar products from specialised systems (Table 1). Nonetheless, when compared with other IAA systems, the GWP 100 was similar. If the CH4 emissions are assumed to be in the lower bound (i.e. 5th percentile) (Table 3), then the GWP 100 from the integrated production would be similar to those of specialised systems. In the upper bound, GWP 100 of the DPS would be comparatively much higher. This suggests that modern specialised aquaculture and sericulture practices have, in general, lower GWP100 than the traditional integrated production.

Nevertheless, LCA models with different allocation ap- proaches and/or different system boundaries should be com- pared with caution. For example, the mulberry DPS fully in- ternalises the burdens of processing the organic waste of the livestock subsystem, which is not always the case in the anal- ysis of intensified systems. Modern intensive aquaculture re- lies on removal of wastes to maintain water quality. When discharge of organic residues to water bodies is modelled,

eutrophication potential is most often assessed-but resulting GHGe are not considered (e.g. Pelletier and Tyedmers 2010; Bosma et al. 2011). This may lead to an underestimation of GHGe as discharges from aquaculture can cause emissions downstream (Ferrón et al. 2007). This truncation could also bias the comparison between cultured fish and terrestrial live- stock for which GHGe from manure are often calculated.

As the DPS was wholly integrated, modelling the livestock and aquaculture system together avoids allocating emissions from waste products used as externally sourced inputs. When waste products are recycled (e.g. detritus as fertiliser) in a subsystem outside of the boundaries of the study, the burdens of field emissions and credit for avoided fertiliser manufacture can be attributed to the user (e.g. Aubin et al. 2006; Aubin et al. 2009) or to the producer (e.g. Nguyen et al. 2010; Williams et al. 2010). The latter approach eases comparison between both IAAs and alternative production systems as waste co-product utilisation and emissions can be integrated in the accounts.

10: Previous studies have identified the utilisation of co-products as a key element of the sustainability of sericulture (Astudillo et al. 2014). This study estimated the net GWP of treating organic waste through the pond or composting. The use of unaerated ponds as a waste management strategy does not seem to be superior when compared with composting, even when lower CH4 estimates are used (Table 2). The N cycling "efficiency" of ponds could be judged superior, because N is in the form of digestible protein, however GWP in our

study turned out to be higher by an order of magnitude. The majority of difference in emissions was a result of the carbon cycle. This illustrates the importance of considering both cy- cles and the C:N ratio of pond inputs, particularly with regard to strategies aimed at mitigating climate change.

Even if the specific system characterised here resulted in estimations of high GWP100, the integration of seri- and aqua- culture could benefit from the use of silkworm pupae as an effective substitute for fishmeal (Makkar et al. 2014). Pupae account for 50% of cocoon dry weight and are often discarded as waste products (Astudillo et al. 2014). Current estimates of global cocoon production (FAO 2014) suggest that approxi- mately 160,000 t of dry pupae would be available annually to substitute fishmeal. The growing body of research on the use of insects as substitutes for fishmeal would complement the analysis of the environmental benefits of moving to plant- based aquaculture feeds (e.g. Samuel-Fitwi et al. 2013).
11: Previous studies have identified the utilisation of co-products as a key element of the sustainability of sericulture (Astudillo et al. 2014). This study estimated the net GWP of treating organic waste through the pond or composting. The use of unaerated ponds as a waste management strategy does not seem to be superior when compared with composting, even when lower CH4 estimates are used (Table 2). The N cycling "efficiency" of ponds could be judged superior, because N is in the form of digestible protein, however GWP in our

study turned out to be higher by an order of magnitude. The majority of difference in emissions was a result of the carbon cycle. This illustrates the importance of considering both cy- cles and the C:N ratio of pond inputs, particularly with regard to strategies aimed at mitigating climate change.

Even if the specific system characterised here resulted in estimations of high GWP100, the integration of seri- and aqua- culture could benefit from the use of silkworm pupae as an effective substitute for fishmeal (Makkar et al. 2014). Pupae account for 50% of cocoon dry weight and are often discarded as waste products (Astudillo et al. 2014). Current estimates of global cocoon production (FAO 2014) suggest that approxi- mately 160,000 t of dry pupae would be available annually to substitute fishmeal. The growing body of research on the use of insects as substitutes for fishmeal would complement the analysis of the environmental benefits of moving to plant- based aquaculture feeds (e.g. Samuel-Fitwi et al. 2013).
12: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.

J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. 14: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.

J Clean Prod 17:354 361 5 Conclusions|Bastviken D, Cole JJ, Pace ML, Van de Bogert MC (2008) Fates of methane from different lake habitats: connecting whole-lake budgets and CH4 emissions. J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. 16: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.
The Zhujiang delta DPS did not employ supplementary aeration. DO concentrations are described as falling to

J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. 17: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.

J Clean Prod 17:354 361 5 Conclusions|Bastviken D, Cole JJ, Pace ML, Van de Bogert MC (2008) Fates of methane from different lake habitats: connecting whole-lake budgets and CH4 emissions. J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. 19: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.
The Zhujiang delta DPS did not employ supplementary aeration. DO concentrations are described as falling to

J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. 20: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.

J Clean Prod 17:354 361 5 Conclusions|Bastviken D, Cole JJ, Pace ML, Van de Bogert MC (2008) Fates of methane from different lake habitats: connecting whole-lake budgets and CH4 emissions. J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. 22: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.

J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. J Clean Prod 17:354 361 5 Conclusions|Bastviken D, Cole JJ, Pace ML, Van de Bogert MC (2008) Fates of methane from different lake habitats: connecting whole-lake budgets and CH4 emissions. J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems. 24: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.
The Zhujiang delta DPS did not employ supplementary aeration. DO concentrations are described as falling to

J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. 25: Early studies of the dyke-pond-system focused on energy flows. CED can function as an effective first screening for GWP10 with the notable exception of agricultural products (Huijbregts et al. 2010). The results presented here support for this view-with the large majority of emissions related to carbon and nitrogen cycling, not to energy input. The large proportion of total GWP attributable to nitrification, deni- trification and methanogenesis suggests that C and N flows should be areas of first priority when assessing the sustainabil- ity of aquaculture systems.

Results presented in this study are limited to the assessment of GHGe. Other impact categories such as energy use or eu- trophication are also of relevance to aquaculture. While we have not presented estimates of eutrophication potential from the DPS, we suggest that the high GWP 100 estimates may to some extent be balanced by significantly lower effluent N loads, as emissions of nutrient-loaded water are limited in the system described (Ruddle and Zhong 1988). Finally, while energy imported in the form of food has an impact on total GHGe through embedded emissions, the use of fossil fuels and non-renewable CED in this system was minimal.
The Zhujiang delta DPS did not employ supplementary aeration. DO concentrations are described as falling to

J Geophys Res 113:G02024. 10.1029/ 2007JG000608 Our study revisited the mulberry DPS, which is a labour- intensive integrated system often considered to be an example of sustainable practices, and we attempted to model GHGe arising from the pond. The model showed GWP 100 from the system as a whole to be dominated by CH4 emissions from the pond. Other on-farm pond emission such as N20 were less important but not negligible. Indirect emissions of the food required for manual labour also played an important role, at up to 22 % of total system GWP100 This suggests that increased mechanisation may reduce total GHGe, particularly for other- wise physically demanding tasks. The study provides a first estimate of possible CH4 emis- sions from DPS ponds, based on existing literature. Results are, however, substantially uncertain and would need to be confirmed by experimental studies. IAA systems similar to DPS still exist in the Mekong Delta, although they account for a small part of total fish production. Further studies could examine emissions from semi-intensive fertilised aquaculture as practised today, especially bighead and silver carp, which are worldwide among the most widely cultured species. Our study and assessment suggest that the use of semi- intensive unaerated fish ponds as waste management and valorisation systems is not superior to, e.g. composting, from the point of view of mitigating GHGe of sericulture and seri- culture by-products. However, closed loop and 'open' aqua- culture systems should be compared with care, as GHGe from effluent waste of open systems are rarely assessed. Finally, the specific case of utilising waste pupae in aquaculture looks promising as they appear to be a very good substitute to fishmeal and are currently underutilised in many silk growing regions.|Blackburn TH (1987) Role and impact of anaerobic microbial processes in aquatic systems.
